ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Butterfly Prawn Vermicelli; FOODON_00002572 (shrimp dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for butterfly prawn vermicelli composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Thai prawn noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of butterfly prawn vermicelli is approximately 350 g, comprising 3–4 whole butterfly-cut prawns arranged on a bed of softened vermicelli noodles with aromatics and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 15152) and Rice vermicelli, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20065), combined by weighted proportion estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Butterfly Prawn Vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168882), combined in an approximate 55:45 prawn-to-noodle ratio by edible weight, with minor adjustments for garlic, sesame oil, and soy sauce sauce components. Values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — GI value from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Butterfly Prawn Vermicelli. GI of 53 is derived from the published GI of boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category) per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values. Prawns contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is dominated by the vermicelli component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 47.25) / 100 ≈ 25.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 13.5 × 3.5 = 47.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 53 (low) but a glycemic load of ~25 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the vermicelli component and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns and elevated sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns contribute approximately 392 mg dietary cholesterol per serving; while dietary cholesterol has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals, the high sodium content from soy sauce is a more significant cardiovascular concern and should be minimised for those with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butterfly Prawn Vermicelli is a Thai-style seafood noodle dish featuring whole prawns that have been butterfly-cut (split along the back and fanned open) and arranged atop a bed of softened rice vermicelli noodles. The dish is typically steamed or assembled with a fragrant sauce of garlic, ginger, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and chilli, then finished with spring onion and sometimes coriander. The prawns are tender and sweet with a slight snap, while the vermicelli absorbs the savoury-umami sauce, resulting in a silky, flavourful texture contrast. The dish is popular in Thai seafood restaurants and has been adopted widely in Singapore's Thai-cuisine dining scene. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate protein source from the prawns (~10 g per 100 g), with carbohydrate energy primarily from the rice vermicelli. Sodium content is elevated due to soy-based seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~53) driven by the rice vermicelli component, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 25. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

Is butterfly prawn vermicelli suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Butterfly Prawn Vermicelli was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp and cooked rice vermicelli in a weighted proportion, with minor sauce component adjustments. GI was derived from the published GI of rice vermicelli per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168882) in an approximate 55:45 ratio by edible weight. Sauce components (garlic, sesame oil, soy sauce) contribute to sodium and fat estimates. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source data for both shrimp and rice vermicelli components. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice vermicelli per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood – Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 25.80. Halal status is assumed based on typical Thai seafood preparation without pork; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. Cholesterol value of 112 mg per 100 g reflects the prawn-dominant composition; full serving (350 g) provides approximately 392 mg dietary cholesterol.
